data.title: Drug giant hit by Trump autism claims bought for record $49bn
data.description:
A US drug giant hit by Donald Trump’s claims linking its paracetamol brand to an increased risk of autism has been bought for $49bn (£37bn) after shares crashed to a record low.

On Monday, Kenvue, which makes the Tylenol paracetamol brand, said it had agreed to be acquired by consumer goods giant Kimberly-Clark, which owns Kleenex, Huggies and Andrex.

The takeover comes after Kenvue shares hit their lowest level on record, with $13bn wiped off the company since the start of the year amid heightened scrutiny over Mr Trump’s claims about Tylenol.

In September, the US president said pregnant women should “fight like hell” not to take the paracetamol drug, urging them to “tough it out” when in pain.

Mr Trump went on to claim that the use of Tylenol during pregnancy could make autism more likely, citing unfounded links. The US president said: “Don’t take Tylenol. Don’t take it.”

The claim prompted a backlash from doctors who argue that it puts pregnant women’s health at risk.

The Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) issued guidance saying there was “no evidence that taking paracetamol during pregnancy causes autism in children”.

In the UK, Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, urged pregnant women not to pay “any attention whatsoever” to Mr Trump’s claims.

Kenvue said it “strongly disagrees with allegations that it does and is deeply concerned about the health risks and confusion this poses for expecting mothers and parents”.

Last week, Robert F Kennedy Jr, the US health secretary, also admitted that there was not “sufficient” evidence to link Tylenol to autism.

However, Kenvue is still facing a lawsuit in Texas over claims that it deceptively marketed its pain medication to pregnant people despite the alleged autism risk.

Kenvue’s $48.7bn takeover marks the largest buyout of a US consumer goods maker ever recorded, ahead of Mars’s $35.9bn takeover of Kellanova last year.

The offer price is at a 46pc premium to Kenvue’s closing price on Friday.

Kenvue, which was spun out of Johnson & Johnson in 2023, has also been drawn into court cases in the UK over its talc powder.

In October, a lawsuit was filed at the High Court over claims that Johnson & Johnson’s baby powder caused illnesses, including ovarian cancer, between 1965 and 2023.

Kenvue is fighting the claims, saying that it expects a judge to conclude its talc-based baby powder does not cause cancer.

The legal case led to claims that the scrutiny risked putting off potential buyers, with companies including Britain’s Haleon having previously been rumoured as possible buyers.

Kenvue kicked off a process to assess selling either parts or the whole company earlier this year in response to pressure from activist investors.

It ousted its chief executive in July as part of a push to prepare for a sale.

data.title: Rising energy bills expose Miliband’s fantasy economics
data.description:
The weather has turned very chilly. There is a thick frost on the ground, and a “beast from the east” may descend at any time.

Still, never mind. Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, promised us that he would take at least £300 off the average energy bill, so at least we can all afford to turn the heating up.

There is just one catch. We learnt on Friday that the energy price cap is going up yet again. In reality, the relentlessly rising cost of energy is exposing Miliband’s fantasy economics – and unless policy changes very soon, that is going to turn into a full-scale crisis.

Labour made very few promises during last year’s election campaign, but on two points it was completely clear. It would not raise any of the three main taxes, and it would cut average power bills by £300 per household.

We will find out next week whether the Chancellor Rachel Reeves can stick to the first one. But the second has already been completely shredded.

Instead of falling as expected, the energy regulator Ofgem surprised everyone by announcing that the price cap would rise again in January, just as the worst of the winter weather hits, and as a cost of living squeeze means that it is harder than ever to afford the cost of heating.

Seriously? Bills are going up again? In the background, the price of oil has been collapsing, falling to just $57 (£43.50) a barrel, down from $80 back in January. Wholesale energy prices have been going down, and are now 4pc lower over the last three months.

If there is a peace deal in Ukraine, prices might come down even further, while, of course, the massive investments the UK has been making in wind and solar power, plus all the money committed to GB Energy, should mean that domestic energy is now more plentiful than ever. And yet the average bill that the households will face is still going up.

In fairness, Ofgem is perfectly open about the reason for the increase. It “is driven by government policy costs and operating costs”, it admitted as it announced the increase.

The cost of building the Sizewell C nuclear power station will add around £1 to the average bill, while the Warm Home Discount will add an estimated 57p. 

The price rise is not driven by global prices, or even by domestic supply and demand. It is entirely the fault of Miliband.

There are two big problems with that. First, he completely misled everyone during the election. 

A reduction in energy bills was not a minor footnote or a passing remark that no one paid very much attention to. It was central to the Labour Party’s entire campaign at last year’s election, repeated again and again.

Despite that, it turns out that neither Miliband nor anyone else in his party had the faintest clue about how to deliver that once they were in power.

Next, and more seriously, his demented green policies are now directly pushing up prices. 

Expanding our nuclear capacity is a good idea, but the fact remains that cumbersome planning restrictions mean that building a new plant costs twice as much as it does in France and four times as much as in South Korea.

If we could build a new nuclear facility at the same price as other countries can, then prices would not need to rise by so much.

Likewise, the Warm Home Discount is just another form of welfare payment to lower income households, and while that might or might not be justifiable, it would surely be better to pay for that through general taxation instead of just whacking a bit more on everyone else’s bills.

It will get worse. The Government is pressing ahead with its “boiler tax”, which will mean extra costs for anyone who is unlucky enough to have their heating system pack up over the winter.

And it is doubling down on wind power, handing out lavish subsidies to the industry that will have to be paid for years to come, even as it becomes alarmingly obvious to everyone that wind is not nearly reliable enough.

The grid can’t cope with it, and costs are not coming under control in the way you would expect (indeed, it is looking as if solar is the far more cost effective option for green energy).

Meanwhile, we are still running down our oil and gas industry in the North Sea, even though there are plentiful resources left to supply our own needs.

In reality, Miliband has lost control of the energy market. Right now, the UK needs a complete reset.

That should mean two major changes. To start with, we need to drop the green targets. The UK has already more than halved its carbon emissions, and we account for less than 1pc of the global total.

At the rate we are deindustrialising – and ExxonMobil closed a major chemicals plant in Scotland only last week, accelerating the decline of manufacturing – it will soon be even lower. 

The priority right now is cheaper energy, not greener. It doesn’t matter whether it comes from wind, solar, gas, nuclear or even fossil fuels – it just needs to cost much, much less.

Next, we should drop the absurd system of price caps and controls. It is not helping anyone. We should have a plentiful supply of energy and then we should leave it to the market to decide which companies deliver it to people’s homes.

We may have only seen a modest rise this week. But no one should kid themselves about what is happening. 

Energy is getting more and more expensive, and the Government is to blame for that – and unless it changes course sooner or later we are all going to freeze to death.

data.title: Dutch back down in stand-off with China over chip factory seizure
data.description:
The Dutch government has backed down in a row for control of a crucial microchip manufacturer to China after a standoff triggered a crisis at European carmakers.

Vincent Karremans, the Dutch economic minister, said the Netherlands dropped an order seizing control of Nexperia to its Chinese owner Wingtech following negotiations with Beijing.

The Netherlands seized control of Nexperia in October, invoking a Cold War-era law, while its chief executive, Zhang Xuezheng, was ousted by a court.

A separate court decision in the Netherlands, that placed Wingtech’s shares in Nexperia under control of an independent administrator remains in effect, Nexperia said.

The Dutch government accused the company’s Chinese owners of “serious governance shortcomings” that threatened the supply of critical microchips to Europe.

According to Dutch media reports, officials also feared that Nexperia’s trade secrets had been stolen by China and it was planning to shift production out of Europe.

The move triggered a crisis in the European car industry, which is heavily reliant on Nexperia’s microchips.

China halted exports of chips to Europe while staff at the company’s Chinese division started refusing instructions from its headquarters on the continent.

Some of Europe’s biggest carmakers warned the supply chain shock could force them to shut their factories.

However, after an intervention this month by Donald Trump, the US president, China said it was prepared to ease an export ban on Nexperia’s technology.

On Tuesday, Mr Karremans said it was now the “right moment to take a constructive step by suspending my order under the Goods Availability Act regarding Nexperia”.

He said: “We are positive about the measures already taken by the Chinese authorities to ensure the supply of chips to Europe and the rest of the world. We see this as a show of goodwill. We will continue to engage in constructive dialogue with the Chinese authorities in the period ahead.”

The crisis has prompted calls for Labour to intervene at a Manchester chip plant that is also controlled by Nexperia.

Nexperia was previously forced to offload a factory it owned in Wales over national security concerns about its Chinese parent company.

Nexperia executives previously denied its ownership of the Welsh plant presented a national security risk. It did not comment on the Manchester claims.

Sir Iain Duncan Smith, a former Conservative Party leader, told The Telegraph last month: “The same rules should apply. I don’t understand why this hasn’t been resolved by the Government.”

A Wingtech spokesman said the Dutch intervention in Nexperia had threatened the “continuity of Nexperia and therefore the economic security of the Netherlands and Europe – which is the exact same argument the Dutch government made previously in support of judicial intervention”.

The spokesman added: “Minister Karremans justified his actions by accusing Nexperia’s CEO of various acts of alleged mismanagement.

“Wingtech strongly rejects these accusations and points out that, to date, no proof has been provided.”

data.title: How the Bank of England can secure its place at the heart of the next monetary revolution
data.description:
This morning, the Bank of England will set out its last consultation on a regime for the regulation of systemic stablecoins. How the UK pursues the regulation of digital money will determine whether London joins the US in leading the next era of financial infrastructure – or watches others set the pace. 

To its credit, the Bank’s position on the regulation of systemic stablecoins has evolved. Perhaps most remarkably, the Governor has explicitly acknowledged that the holding of money can be separated from the provision of credit – a profound shift in how we think about the structure of the current financial system. 

With the 180 day UK-US Taskforce for Markets of the Future, the UK has a rare opportunity to shape how innovation in money can preserve financial centre competitiveness.

The Governor has said that stablecoins aspiring to serve as money must be backed by “risk-free” assets. That principle is sound, but not one applied to traditional bank deposits. The Governor himself recognises that commercial bank deposits – with fractional backing – are not risk-free. 

In fact, stablecoins backed one-for-one by central bank cash reserves and other (credit) risk-free assets are less risky than uninsured deposits – and their regulatory treatment should reflect this.

The Bank’s willingness to allow a portion of reserves to be held in high quality liquid assets (HQLA) like gilts, rather than exclusively in central bank, deposits is welcome progress. A sensible approach would see a significant majority – perhaps over 80 per cent – in HQLA.

This has three advantages. First, it creates a viable pathway for issuers to transition between the FCA (non-systemic) and Bank of England (systemic) stablecoin regimes, without the need to fundamentally change business models. Second, it introduces new demand for short-dated gilts, supporting the UK Government’s funding base and lowering borrowing costs. Third, it aligns the UK regime with that of other major jurisdictions, making the country a more attractive destination for stablecoin innovation.

Concerns have been raised that in a crisis a run on a stablecoin issuer could trigger a fire-sale of gilts. If that’s the case, the Governor’s suggestion that the Bank could offer Emergency Liquidity Assistance (ELA) is one way to address this. 

The Bank also looks set to propose caps on stablecoin holdings, supposedly to guard against a “precipitous” drain of deposits from banks into digital money. 

Other jurisdictions offer a useful test case. In the United States, the dollar denominated stablecoin USDC has operated for nearly six years without evidence of a destabilising flight from retail deposits. In fact, in the 70 months of data from USDC, we observe that both retail deposits and USDC’s market cap have increased during that time. 

Empirical evidence suggests neither caps, nor a ban on stablecoins paying interest are justified. By proposing both, which no other jurisdiction has deemed necessary, the UK risks putting the brakes on innovation. 

The focus should be on facilitating modernisation, not privileging legacy systems. The Bank’s proposals sit awkwardly with both the Government’s competitiveness strategy and firms’ obligations under the Consumer Duty. 

Furthermore, it is unclear how caps on holdings would be implemented once a stablecoin transitions from the FCA’s regime for non-systemic stablecoins to the Bank of England’s regime once it becomes systemic - would there be forced liquidations? Second, the caps proposed are so low that it would make stablecoins unusable for wholesale settlement in tokenised markets. 

The rationale for caps is therefore weak, and industry needs clarity on when – and on what basis – those caps would be lifted. The Governor has said it is important to “think through the implications of change before going ahead”. But there is also risk in excessive caution. We should not want the UK to deliver a regulatory framework for stablecoins that deters innovation, at the same time as other markets move ahead.

The US has legislated to provide regulatory clarity on stablecoins, and the market has responded with around 80 different announcements of companies building in this space. 

The Bank’s evolving approach to stablecoins includes some strong and welcome progress. However, more can be done. In short, with prudence and ambition, a competitive, well-regulated stablecoin regime can strengthen financial stability and secure London’s place at the heart of the next monetary revolution. 

Faryar Shirzad is Chief Policy Officer at Coinbase

data.title: John Lewis considers opening new stores for first time in nearly a decade
data.description:
John Lewis is considering opening new department stores for the first time in nearly a decade.

Peter Ruis, the managing director of John Lewis, said expansion was “definitely something we are looking at” and suggested the company was plotting moves into new parts of the country.

He told The Guardian: “The store is a perfect invention, and we’ve seen only too well, coming back from Covid, how people have gravitated back to the stores.”

Any new launch would be the chain’s first since its shop in Cheltenham, Gloucestershire, opened in 2018. A spokesman said there were no immediate plans for new store openings.

It comes as John Lewis seeks to reinvigorate the business after closing 16 sites and cutting thousands of jobs following a pandemic-induced slump in sales.

The 161-year-old employee-owned department store chain lost money for three years in a row before returning to profit in 2023.

Mr Ruis, who previously served as John Lewis’s fashion boss, returned to the company last year to lead turnaround efforts.

The retailer has set out plans to spend £800m refurbishing its 36 remaining stores by 2029. The flagship John Lewis store on Oxford Street reopened last year following a major overhaul.

Bosses had previously planned to convert almost half the floor space into offices. Still, they opted to retain all six floors after a stronger-than-expected rebound in shopping following the pandemic.

Other locations that have benefited from a facelift include those in High Wycombe, Buckinghamshire, and Cheadle, Greater Manchester. Meanwhile, the store in Bluewater Shopping Centre in Kent recently reopened after a £10m makeover that included a new “gifting emporium”.

Mr Ruis said he was aiming to ensure that John Lewis’s physical stores remained relevant to shoppers. He said: “We are getting rid of the old stuffy department store and replacing it with something more experiential.”

Innovations include opening Waterstones bookshops in-store and introducing new brands such as Topshop and Charlotte Tilbury.

John Lewis also plans to expand its partnership with Uber Eats, allowing customers to order beauty products and gifts directly to their door.

It comes ahead of a crucial trading period for John Lewis, which is relying on an increase in Christmas sales to balance the books after its losses fell to £88m in the first half of the year.

The retailer has previously blamed Labour’s tax increases, including higher National Insurance contributions and a new packaging levy, for the losses.

However, it has targeted better results in the second half of the year.

Mr Ruis said: “I think we’re optimistic, but, you know, it’s a difficult market. It’s difficult for the customer.”

data.title: Chinese EV giant to build one of Europe’s biggest car factories
data.description:
A Chinese electric vehicle (EV) maker is scouting for locations to build one of Europe’s biggest car factories.

Great Wall Motor is looking at sites in Spain and Hungary along with other countries as it targets a production output of 300,000 vehicles per year locally.

Assuming most of the carmaker’s production is concentrated there, the factory would be one of the largest in Europe.

Britain’s largest plant, run by Nissan in Sunderland, has a maximum capacity of 600,000 but in practice has been producing far fewer vehicles than this recently.

Volkswagen’s Wolfsburg plant in Germany is the biggest in Europe, with a maximum theoretical capacity of around 900,000 per year.

Amid brutal competition on pricing at home, Chinese carmakers have been piling into the European market where they have been able to undercut more established brands.

For example, BYD has increased its sales from about 36,000 to 138,000 in the first 10 months of the year while SAIC Motor, the owner of MG, has gone from about 198,000 to 250,000. The European Automobile Manufacturers’ Association (ACEA) does not publish figures for Great Wall Motor.

Parker Shi, the president of Great Wall Motor International, said his company had a target to export one million cars globally by 2030. The target includes 300,000 cars in Europe.

Mr Shi told Reuters, which reported the announcement: “That’s why we’re speeding up the European strategy. Everything needs to speed up.”

In the UK, Great Wall Motor is selling the electric ORA 03, a five-door hatchback, for upwards of £21,000, along with the Haval Jolion Pro compact SUV for about £22,000 and the POER300 pickup truck for about £31,500.

By comparison, electric versions of the Mini Cooper and the Vauxhall Corsa – which are similar in terms of performance – start at around £28,000 and £27,000 respectively.

But as Chinese firms’ sales have grown, so has anxiety in European governments about the threat to local jobs and regional brands such as VW and Renault.

The companies have stolen a march in particular on EVs, which they are able to produce at significantly lower cost than their European counterparts.

There are also concerns about the massive amounts of state aid lavished by Beijing and Chinese local authorities on their own car manufacturers through cheap loans, vehicle mandates, charging infrastructure and grants.

Against that backdrop, the European Union last year imposed tariffs on imported Chinese EVs.

That has spurred the companies to look at local production lines. However, to avoid tariffs, they will have to meet certain “rules of origin” requirements that demand more than simply shipping parts to Europe and putting them together.

Mr Shi said Great Wall Motor was keeping a close eye on EU policies, including emission rules designed to spur the sales of EVs.

“It’s going to be a huge investment for a long term,” he added.

data.title: Gulf royalty strikes deal to build thousands of London homes
data.description:
A Dubai-based property developer owned by Middle Eastern royalty has struck a deal to build thousands of homes alongside the River Thames.

Arada, co-founded by Sheikh Sultan bin Ahmed Al Qasimi of Sharjah, a city in the United Arab Emirates, and Saudi Arabia’s Prince Khaled bin Alwaleed bin Talal, has acquired an 80pc stake in Thameside West, a 47-acre development project in east London.

The scheme is set to deliver at least 5,000 homes near Royal Victoria Dock, and is estimated to be worth £2.5bn once completed.

It signals the growing influx of Middle Eastern money into London, particularly in the property sector.

Arada acquired the stake in Thameside West from London developer Keystone, paying an upfront fee of £225m alongside a pledge to invest a further £100m.

Keystone will own the remaining 20pc of the site, which is nearly twice the size of Hudson Yards in Manhattan, New York.

The acquisition is also one of the biggest land deals in London since 2020, giving the capital a much-needed boost after a recent slump in demand.

Just 5,933 new-build private homes were sold during the first three quarters of 2025, according to findings published by consultancy Molior London last month, with sales “weak across all local markets and at every price point”.

Dwindling sales have also led to a drop in construction, with activity hampered by high costs and uncertainty prompted by this month’s Budget.

Recent estimates show that just 15,000 to 20,000 private homes are to be under construction in London by the end of 2026.

‘Dubai model’

However, Ahmed Alkhoshaibi, the chief executive and co-founder of Arada, is confident that demand will recover.

“We will build and it will come,” he said. “That’s been the Dubai model. We will build even if there isn’t demand upfront.”

Mr Alkhoshaibi added that it is “the right time” to invest. He said: “It’s always the best time to buy when the price of the land is down as a developer.

“To create spaces that are on another level, you need to buy at the right price. Right now, London is down, but we’re confident it will be up sooner than later.”

It comes after Arada bought London housing developer Regal for £230m in September, with the aim of building 30,000 homes in the capital over the next three years.

During the 1990s, Prince Khaled was known for enjoying a lavish lifestyle, reportedly owning 200 luxury cars.

However, the guilt he experienced from taking part in a trophy hunt in South Africa in the latter part of the decade prompted him to adopt a more pared-back lifestyle – selling off his collection, becoming vegan and driving just one car, a Tesla.

His family also owns nearly 50pc of the Four Seasons international hotel brand, with the other half owned by Bill Gates.

data.title: Office landlord hit with ‘outrageous’ backdated tax bill
data.description:
The founder of a co-working space has warned that his business faces collapse over an “outrageous” backdated business rates bill.

Hugo Warner, co-founder of The Fisheries shared office space in Hackney, east London, said his business had been “singled out” with a “highly unfair” £400,000 charge dating back to 2023.

The case has prompted fears of a stealth tax raid on shared offices in Wednesday’s Budget.

Mr Warner claimed that the Valuation Office Agency (VOA), which handles business rates decisions, had chosen to re-rate the shared office building as a single establishment, rather than charging it as separate units.

John Webber, the head of ratings at Colliers, the property agency that is advising Mr Warner, accused the VOA of “arbitrary and outrageous” behaviour, “picking on one small company because it thinks it won’t be able to fight back”.

When charged separately, the individual tenants were able to claim small business rates relief, with the administration of the taxes handled by the building operator.

But the change has left him facing a vast historical tax claim that threatens the future of his business. If he is forced to pay it, he said: “The Fisheries London would become insolvent and go into administration.”

Mr Warner, 56, who founded coffee chain Benugo, has launched a legal challenge against the decision in what could become a test case for the industry. It comes amid fears that more shared office groups could be hit with sizeable business rates claims from local authorities.

In a letter sent by Rachel Reeves to Mr Warner’s MP, the Labour backbencher Dame Meg Hillier, the Chancellor defended the approach of the valuations agency.

Ms Reeves wrote that the agency had concluded that “most serviced offices will need to be assessed as a single property, unless clear evidence demonstrates a need to split”.

She said decisions were being made on a “case-by-case” basis, but that the agency was looking to “clarify the application of case law on serviced offices”. She said the agency would then “consider its approach to the rest of the sector”.

The Chancellor added that by applying a single rating to the building, that falls on the operator, it would mean that the small and micro-businesses working inside the office “face no business rates at all”.

Indirect tax threat

However, Mr Warner said that any changes to rates on shared office companies would ultimately be passed on to the renters of flexible office space. “Indirectly, it would be a tax on small and microbusinesses,” he said.

He also warned that many shared office providers risked being caught in the Government’s new “high-value” multiplier for business rates, which will hit businesses with a rateable value of over £500,000. It has been branded a new “super tax” by critics, although the Government has insisted just 1pc of buildings will be affected.

Colliers accused the VOA of a “new bid to raise extra revenue”. Mr Webber said if the agency was successful, the Government could consider “rolling this damaging policy out against the serviced office sector more widely”.

He added: “This result would be a massive hit to providers of flex space, to small businesses, and to the economy as a whole.”

Last week, 60 office operators – with 27,000 tenants – wrote to the Chancellor warning that a wholesale change would pose an “existential threat” to the sector.

A spokesman for the VOA said: “Developments in case law have meant that we have had to review the way serviced offices are assessed. As a result, many may now need to be treated as a single property rather than individual units, depending on their contractual arrangements.

“We understand this could have a financial impact on operators and we are engaging with industry representatives to discuss our approach. However, we are obliged to apply the law based on the facts in each individual case.”

data.title: Best regular savings accounts: Today’s latest rates
data.description:
A regular savings account requires you to commit to paying in a certain amount of money each month, usually between £10 and £500. In return, the bank gives you a higher interest rate than you would normally get with other types of accounts.

Regular savers are usually fixed-term accounts which last for a year.

In this guide, Telegraph money will cover:

  • How we determine the best rates
  • The best regular savings accounts
  • Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing a regular savings account
  • Regular savings FAQs

How we determine the best rates

Telegraph Money’s Best Buy tables show the best savings rates widely available in the market. This means certain accounts are excluded, including those that require minimum deposits of more than £25,000 or are available only to local or existing customers.

The data in these tables is provided by Savings Data Limited and is compiled using automated tracker tools and updates from savings providers. Savings Data Limited then manually checks the information and enters it into a database that feeds the live tables, which update daily.

The savings accounts shown are protected by the Financial Services Compensation Scheme. The information in this article is intended for information purposes and should not be taken as endorsement or advice.

The best regular savings account

Use the table below to discover the best regular savings accounts for you.

For other savings options that allow you to access your money, see our guides to the best easy-access savings accounts, along with the best notice accounts and best current accounts.

You might be able to find higher rates by locking your cash away – don’t miss our guide to the best fixed-rate bonds.

Plus, don’t miss our guide to the best cash Isas, which shows the top options for easy-access, notice, regular and fixed-rates – where your savings interest is all tax-free.

Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing a regular savings account

Mark Hicks, of investment and savings platform Hargreaves Lansdown, said: “Regular savers can have a very catchy headline rate. But quite often, the maximum amount of money you can deposit is low. Banks may attract you in with a high interest rate and then you realise you can save £5,000.”

“They are very good if you want to deposit small amounts of money and if you’re new to saving,” he added.

And Caitlyn Eastell, of Moneyfactscompare.co.uk said: “Regular savings accounts are an ideal choice for those wanting to receive a higher rate of interest or wish to develop and maintain a regular savings habit.

“However, many regular savings accounts don’t give much flexibility on the amount that can be deposited each month and often have minimum or maximum monthly payments.”

Regular savings FAQs

Yes, you can open more than one account. Regular savers are for making smaller payments, so there is nothing stopping you from opening several to earn more interest on your money.

Some accounts will have a minimum number of payments you need to make each year – often 10 or 11. Some will require you to pay in a certain amount every month.

Yes, money held in regular savings accounts is protected as long as your account is with a UK-regulated bank or building society, the money is protected under the Financial Services Compensation Scheme up to £85,000 per institution.

Yes, you can open a regular savings account for your children. Check our guide to the best children’s savings accounts to see the latest rates.

It takes time to build up the amount of money you have in the account because you can only make small deposits.

It is also worth bearing in mind that the interest you get will be less than the headline rate. This is how it works when the money is saved monthly, rather than one lump sum.

A regular savings account can help develop good saving habits. If you are only able to put a small amount of money away each month and would prefer a short-term arrangement, then it might be the best situation for you.

A regular savings account gives you considerably better interest rates than standard accounts, rewarding you for making frequent savings over a year or so.

However, there are generally rigid terms and conditions. The amount you can save is capped and your interest rate might be reduced if you don’t save every month or if you need to make a withdrawal. Many accounts don’t allow early withdrawals whatsoever.

You usually need to have another product with the same bank – usually a current account.

The rate generally lasts for a year, meaning your money is moved to an account with lower interest rates at the end of the term.

Some accounts have variable interest rates – meaning the provider can change the rates whenever they want – so it’s important to keep an eye on messages from the provider and switch, if the rates drop too low.

Any tax due on interest you earn over the personal savings allowance is generally calculated by the bank. Rachel Springall, of Moneyfactscompare.co.uk, said: “Banks will notify HMRC [HM Revenue & Customs] of the annual interest received and consumers’ records will be updated based on this. Consumers can contact HMRC by telephone or in writing before this is done. Any charges will be notified by way of a calculation and tax code change.”

“There are many savers out there who have been vocal to not want their tax code changed, so they would need to pay HMRC a lump sum, and thus it may be beneficial for them to proactively contact HMRC with their details,” she added.

data.title: Domino’s boss departs after warning of ‘peak pizza’
data.description:
The chief executive of Domino’s has stepped down unexpectedly after warning that Britain was hitting peak pizza.

Andrew Rennie, who ran the chain for two years, will leave the business immediately after unveiling ambitious plans to tilt Domino’s towards fried chicken amid a slump in pizza demand.

Nicola Frampton, the chain’s current chief operating officer, will take over as caretaker chief executive while Ian Bull, the chairman, and senior director Natalia Barsegiyan search for a permanent replacement.

Mr Rennie’s exit comes just days after he warned that the UK’s pizza market was approaching saturation, with a supply glut of chains pushing diners towards burgers and fried chicken.

In response to the slowdown, Mr Rennie announced plans to start selling fried chicken from 1,400 British Domino’s stores through a new brand known as “Chick ‘n’ Dip” as well as looking for a new fast food chain to acquire.

On Tuesday, the pizza chain added that it will hold back from making any new acquisitions until a permanent chief executive is in place.

Domino’s, which opened its first UK store in Luton in 1985, has suffered a sharp slowdown in sales over the past two years which has dented its profits.

Its problems have been made worse by higher energy costs and the financial hit from Rachel Reeves’s increase of employers’ costs. Other pizza chains, such as Pizza Hut, have also struggled with the economic headwinds.

It has seen the FTSE 250 company’s share price slump by 51pc over the past year, as the stock has become one of the most shorted on the London stock market.

In September, hedge fund Browning West publicly urged Mr Bull to halt Mr Rennie’s acquisition plans.

The hedge fund, which owns a 5pc stake in Domino’s, instead called on the company to launch a £100m share buyback to return more money to investors. Domino’s said it now plans to “review its capital allocation priorities”.

Mr Rennie started out as a Domino’s franchisee in Australia when he was 26 years old before he was recruited by the company’s head office.

Ms Frampton previously worked at Deloitte and William Hill before joining Domino’s in 2021.

data.title: Inflation calculator: how the latest rate will affect your savings
data.description:
This calculator shows the current rate of Consumer Prices Index (CPI) inflation will affect the return on your savings.

Inflation calculator

How to use this calculator

Type in the amount you have in a savings account or cash Isa, and the interest rate you’re currently receiving.

Press the “Calculate” button to see the result.

How it works

This calculator works by figuring out how much interest your savings will earn over the course of a year – this assumes interest is paid annually, and the interest rate and amount of savings remains the same.

This is pitted against the most recent CPI measure of inflation, as reported by the Office for National Statistics.

Assuming inflation remains steady, it then shows the value your savings will lose in real terms due to inflationary price increases.

The calculator will also show you what the cumulative impact of these losses will be for each of the next five years.

What is this calculator useful for?

The calculator can help you gauge whether or not it’s worth switching to a savings account or cash Isa offering a higher rate of interest.

This is particularly important when inflation is high, but in general it’s a good idea to make sure you’re taking advantage of the best savings rates to reduce the effect of price increases. If you don’t, put simply, your money won’t buy as much as it could the previous year.

You can also use the calculator to see how much of a difference it would make to switch savings accounts for a better rate.

For example, someone with £20,000 in an account paying today’s average easy-access rate, according to Moneyfacts, of 2.51pc would make a “real terms” loss of £210.42 after a year because of inflation. They would earn £502 in interest, but their pot would effectively be worth just £19,789.58 after a year when adjusted for inflation.

Switching to the top easy-access savings rate which, according to today’s tables showing the best easy access savings accounts, is from Cahoot and pays 5pc, they’d make an inflationary increase of £270.27, based on actual interest of £1,000.

data.title: Steve Reed lines up administrators for Thames Water
data.description:
Ministers have appointed City insolvency advisers to prepare for the potential collapse of Thames Water.

Steve Reed, the Environment Secretary, has selected FTI Consulting to help draw up contingency plans in the event that the struggling utility giant is placed into a taxpayer-backed special administration regime.

It comes as Thames Water, which has 16 million customers across London and the South East, teeters on the brink of collapse as it struggles under a £17bn debt pile.

Its future was cast into fresh doubt in June after private equity firm KKR pulled out of a £4bn rescue deal.

The company’s creditors have put forward an alternative £17bn rescue plan, but the bailout is contingent on regulators agreeing to waive historical fines.

Mr Reed has previously suggested this would not happen, making a government intervention appear increasingly likely. It is estimated that a state bailout would cost the taxpayer as much as £4bn.

The Environment Secretary in June said Labour was stepping up preparations to temporarily nationalise Thames Water. A government source said the move was a natural consequence of this.

The move to appoint FTI, which was first reported by Sky News, places the consulting firm in prime position to act as the company’s administrator should it collapse into insolvency, though the appointment of a special administrator is ultimately a decision for the court.

A Government spokesman said: “The Government will always act in the national interest on these issues.

“The company remains financially stable, but we have stepped up our preparations and stand ready for all eventualities, including applying for a special administration regime if that were to become necessary.”

Thames is currently locked in discussions with its creditors and regulator Ofwat over a new rescue deal for the company.

Creditors have been pushing for ministers to intervene to wipe out historical fines, saying a “regulatory reset” was required to ensure the utility is financially sustainable.

Thames was handed a record £123m fine in May and creditors say it could be in line for penalties of more than £1bn relating to environmental and pollution failings.

The £17bn rescue plan involves injecting £5bn in new equity and debt and writing off billions of pounds in existing debt.

A Thames Water spokesman said: “Our focus remains on a holistic and fundamental recapitalisation, delivering a market-led solution which includes targeting investment grade credit ratings and returning the company to a stable financial foundation.

“Constructive discussions with our many stakeholders continue.”

data.title: Starmer’s AI drive ‘will lead to hosepipe bans’
data.description:
Sir Keir Starmer’s artificial intelligence (AI) plans risk leading to hosepipe bans and could drive up water bills, a government advisory body has warned.

A report from the Government Digital Sustainability Alliance (GDSA) said that placing major data centres in water-stressed areas could lead to “increased water bills [and] rationing”, adding it risked “potentially exacerbating social conflicts”.

Last week, the Prime Minister trumpeted a series of promised investments in AI data centres, including the construction of Britain’s most powerful AI supercomputer and a new “AI growth zone” in Northumberland.

The string of investments by US tech giants, which coincided with Donald Trump’s state visit, led to predictions of a “big bang” for the British AI industry.

However, the GDSA, a group set up by the Department for Environment, Food & Rural Affairs, claimed that Britain’s water supplies were not adequately prepared for a boom in data centres, which can require large quantities of water to cool computer systems.

It warned that the tech projects were a “blind spot” in official water forecasting, and that utilities were not planning for a surge in data centre infrastructure.

“The siting of water-intensive infrastructure in already water-stressed areas, particularly those with existing socio-economic vulnerabilities, raises significant environmental justice concerns,” it said.

“This practice could disproportionately impact local communities, potentially leading to increased water bills, rationing or reduced environmental flows.”

It warned there was a conflict between the Government’s AI dreams and Britain’s ability to manage water supplies.

“The rapid expansion of AI presents a profound challenge to the UK’s water security and environmental sustainability,” it said.

“The evidence demonstrates that AI’s substantial water footprint, primarily driven by data centre cooling and energy generation, is escalating at an alarming rate and is largely unaccounted for in current national water resource planning.

“This critical oversight, coupled with existing and projected water deficits in the UK, creates a significant risk of exacerbating water stress, leading to potential social and environmental conflicts.”

Source of conflict

The country is already facing pressure on its future water supply. England will require an extra five billion litres of water a day by 2050, according to the Environment Agency.

The GDSA, which is made up of business executives, academics and industry groups, exists to give the Government advice when developing new policies. Its members views do not necessarily reflect those of the Government.

The report by Chris Kenny, the co-chair of one of the GDSA’s panel groups, said measures recently proposed by water companies to improve Britain’s water supplies by 2050 did not account for the growth in data centres.

Data centres are crucial to AI and use large amounts of energy. Last week, Microsoft pledged to invest £22bn in Britain, the majority of which will go towards new infrastructure.

Thames Water warned that its water supplies risked being stressed by more than 100 new data centres planned in the regions it covers.

The tech industry has disputed that data centres risk adding to Britain’s water problems. Industry body techUK said in a report last month that the majority of new data centres used so-called closed loop-cooling systems that do not require any external water supplies.

Plans to support the AI industry have led to other conflicts within the Government.

Peter Kyle, the Business Secretary, has championed a proposed giant data centre on Teesside that would conflict with a proposed hydrogen plant believed to be supported by Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary.

A government spokesman said: “Data centres are part of the vital infrastructure behind the digital technology that powers our businesses and everyday lives, and we need substantial investment to harness AI’s transformative potential.

“The planning system takes into account water scarcity and local plans themselves should take into account the long-term implications for water supply. We’re also unlocking £104bn in water infrastructure investment, which includes improving water supplies around data centres.”

data.title: Cheap Chinese wind turbines to flood British waters
data.description:
British waters could be flooded with cheap Chinese wind turbines in the future because the cost of Beijing building the machines has plummeted so fast, a new study has warned.

Offshore turbines can now be built in China for roughly a third of the price of those produced by companies such as Denmark’s Vestas Wind Systems or Siemens Gamesa, which are major suppliers to Britain, the Oxford Institute of Energy Studies (OIES) said.

This means British offshore wind farms may be forced to embrace Chinese-made machines instead of European models because they are cheaper.

Michal Meiden, the institute’s head of China energy research, said Beijing had achieved an “astonishing” reduction in the cost of turbines, reducing them from $1,200 (£915) per kilowatt in 2019 to less than $420 today.

In Europe, by contrast, turbine prices were last year still above $1,000 per kilowatt – a figure that would make a mockery of Energy Secretary Ed Miliband’s pledge to start manufacturing turbines in the UK.

“In Europe there is a concern that if these cost advantages stem from state subsidies, short-term gains would be undermined by longer-term ruin for the European industry,” Meiden said.

The report also warned that only China may have the manufacturing capacity to supply all the machines the UK and Europe want to install to meet targets for renewable energy.

Britain has about 3,000 offshore wind turbines already installed plus another 9,200 onshore – but thousands more much larger machines are planned over the next five years.

The costs of such machines have, however, surged due to rising prices for steel and other components, with European manufacturers hit especially hard.

The OIES researchers warned that Europe might find it impossible to meet its targets for expanding renewables unless it embraced Chinese machines.

“Europe is unlikely to meet its 2030 targets for offshore wind without additional capacity and clearer regulatory structures,” it said.

John Constable, professor of energy policy at the University of Austin in Texas, said China’s wind turbines were cheap because the factories producing them are powered by burning coal.

He said: “China’s energy supply is 87pc fossil fuel, and over 60pc is low-cost coal, so it is not surprising that they can make wind turbines more cheaply than European manufacturers

“In Europe the electricity supply is now dominated by renewables, making costs so high that overall consumption is dropping like a stone.”

The institute’s research comes amid concerns that hi-tech Chinese products may have covert remote access capabilities that could be activated during a geopolitical crisis to cause significant disruption.

The UK has already banned Huawei components from communications networks.

There have also been more recent reports of US solar farms discovering kill switches that could be activated by cellular radios embedded within Chinese-made components such as inverters.

A spokesman for the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero said: “Offshore wind is crucial to making Britain a clean energy superpower, protecting households from volatile fossil-fuel prices and lowering energy bills for good.

“As an open economy, we welcome foreign trade and investment where it supports growth and jobs in the UK, meets our stringent legal and regulatory requirements, and does not compromise our national security.”

data.title: Britain’s Victorian railway embankments are collapsing. And it could cost us billions
data.description:
When a London-bound express train derailed near the village of Shap in Cumbria earlier this month after ploughing into a landslide, ministers insisted that such incidents were vanishingly rare.

What they failed to mention was that landslips on the railway have reached an all-time high, as a stormier and rainier climate threatens to damage hundreds of miles of embankments dating back to Victorian times.

With Britain’s trains busier than ever, fears are now growing that these centuries-old railway embankments cutting through the countryside could pose a serious risk to passengers – and cost billions to make safer.

Britain’s creaking railways have many problems – from overcrowding and late timetables to poor Wi-Fi and constant signal failures. Yet the dangers posed by landslides and poorly maintained embankments can have fatal consequences.

In 2020, three people died in Stonehaven, Scotland when the lead carriages of a high-speed train en route to Aberdeen plunged down a bank after hitting a landslide triggered by torrential rain.

The accident was the first to kill a passenger since 2007 and the first fatal crash caused by a train hitting a landslip since 1995.

Although last month’s Cumbria derailment was the first major landslide incident since Stonehaven, there are concerns that Britain’s railway system is poorly equipped to detect landslides and prevent further derailments.

Alarmingly, only a minority of landslides are detected by monitors set up to provide a warning when they occur.

Just 8pc of landslips – 13 of 147 – were detected via remote sensors last year, despite £33m having been spent on monitors, a freedom of information request to the Office of Rail and Road found.

In the case of the Cumbria derailment, the Rail Accident Investigation Branch (RAIB) has said that one of its chief concerns was the “effectiveness of on-site monitoring equipment”.

The spike in incidents and low detection rates has begun to raise concerns about whether remote monitoring can protect passengers – and the costs of upgrading the system if not.

Currently, the vast majority of incidents are reported by train drivers, maintenance crews and engineers.

And drivers are becoming increasingly concerned about the growing risks from landslides, some railway chiefs say.

Tricia Williams, managing director of Northern, Britain’s second-biggest train operator, said after Cumbria she was forced to reassure her drivers and train crew that it was safe for services to operate across the north of England.

Ms Williams said Northern had suffered its own near miss at Grange-over-Sands in Cumbria when the track collapsed ahead of a train as an embankment was washed away.

“We have to recognise it’s a risk, but we’ve got to keep the railway moving. The key is the monitoring equipment,” she said.

“I think there was a monitor at Shap, but there needs to be a certain number of monitors triggered. The message to my team is that they are the eyes and ears as well, and that if they see things that they are worried about, report them.”

Expensive alternative

The alternative to driver monitoring is the saturation of the entire 10,000-mile rail network with remote sensors.

But coupled with measures to shore up thousands of miles of slopes, that could cost billions.

Gareth Evans, head of Rail Technology at Network Rail, said such an approach would be so expensive as to be impractical.

He said: “We have to take a risk-based approach to understanding the earthwork assets because there’s so many of them it would not be affordable for the taxpayer to deploy instruments on every single metre.”

The Aslef train drivers’ union said that, whatever the bill, everything possible must be done to enhance safety and avoid a repeat of Stonehaven.

Mick Whelan, Aslef general secretary, said: “Stonehaven casts a long shadow and we must ensure that such an accident never happens in this country again.

“While we understand that there are many cuttings, embankments, bridges, and viaducts on Britain’s railway network, it is vital that Network Rail has the money to ensure that landslips, and other failures, do not happen.”

In the recent Cumbria incident, an Avanti Pendolino tilting train ran into a mudslide immediately south of a summit near Shap, which is the highest point on the West Coast Main Line in England.

While the train was operating at only 83mph, two-thirds of its top speed, and the landslide was shallow – estimates suggest a depth of just a few feet – the impact was sufficient to lift the wheels of the leading carriage off the rails and into the gap between the tracks.

It then ran for more than half a kilometre before coming to a standstill. Extensive damage was caused to the train and track, though just four of the 95 people on board were hurt.

At the Pendolino’s top speed of 125mph, and with a full load of 600-plus passengers, however, the outcome could have been very different.

The RAIB’s preliminary report said a drainage ditch has been flooded because of the heavy rain, leading the slope of the embankment to become too muddy and causing the landslide.

Yet, worryingly, monitors were in operation in the area but seemingly failed to detect the landslip. A Network Rail spokesman declined to comment further on concerns regarding monitoring equipment.

Sensors failure

Network Rail, which is due to be merged with nationalised train operators to create Great British Railways (GBR), is responsible for more than 190,000 individual “earthworks” – which include embankments and soil and rock cuttings.

Rock cuttings are the most likely to suffer some kind of landslide, but they can be covered up to catch any falling stones.

It is soil cuttings that have become the main focus of monitoring, which over 20 years has progressed from visual inspections through instruments drilled into the ground to lower-cost sensor technologies that directly alert rail control rooms to any ground movement.

Simon Abbott, Network Rail head of geotechnics, said that in cases where “sensors are taken out by a large failure” there are many examples of them “having worked really well”.

However, he said that monitoring is “not a silver bullet”.

“Sending people out is a resource we don’t really have at our disposal any more, so technology is playing more of a part,” he said.

Mr Abbott added: “You could put more investment into any aspect of that overall approach and see value, but it’s how you use an appropriate level of funding across the spectrum.

“We’ve got a huge amount of work that we’ve identified where we can justify intervening, and we’ve also got sites where we want to strengthen how we manage risk.”

Network Rail has also placed greater focus on weather forecasting in the wake of Stonehaven, with trains drastically slowed down in areas experiencing heavy rain.

Following the recent Cumbria incident, Heidi Alexander, the Transport Secretary, pledged that safety would remain the number one priority for the industry through nationalisation.

She said: “It is right that Network Rail are making sure they shape the way in which they are spending money to address the changing climate.

“I do want proper monitoring to be in place to make sure that we reduce disruption to a minimum and that we can keep our railways operating as safely as possible.

“I don’t ever want to be in a position again where I’m doing a broadcast round and find out that there has been a train derailment.”

However, Richard Holden, the Conservative transport spokesman, said he is concerned that Labour’s creation of GBR will erect barriers to the work needed to combat landslides.

He said: “We want to see more and better monitoring, but you get that through innovation. And to get the Treasury to invest in innovation you’ve got to have a growing rail sector.

“You need a railway which is operating busily so that the risk of not doing something can be calculated in financial terms. If stopping a popular service running is going to involve a huge amount of cost, it’s going to get national attention, as the Shap incident did.

“But as the railway is brought under state control, who is going to be fighting for resources at a time when the Government is being squeezed on multiple different fronts?”
